entries.tutorialTitle: A chicken first aid kit
entries.tutorialSubtitle: What to keep on hand for minor injuries and common ailments.
entries.tutorialExcerpt: A chicken that has been pecked, cut, or is showing early signs of illness needs attention before a vet visit is possible. A small kit with antiseptic spray, a wound dressing, and an isolation pen keeps options open and prevents minor injuries from escalating.

entries.tutorialTitle: Cold-hardy poultry breeds for a UK winter
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Which breeds manage best when the ground freezes and the runs go muddy.
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Not every breed manages a UK winter with the same equanimity. Single-combed Mediterranean breeds stop laying in cold weather and suffer frostbite on their combs. The northern utility breeds were developed for exactly the conditions a British November delivers.

entries.tutorialTitle: Comb honey using Ross rounds
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Getting round sections of cut comb from a busy summer colony.

entries.tutorialTitle: Deep cleaning a rabbit colony pen
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Stripping and disinfecting the colony pen to break parasite and pathogen cycles.
entries.tutorialExcerpt: A deep clean of the colony pen removes accumulated parasite eggs, coccidial oocysts, and bacterial contamination that routine spot-cleaning misses. entries.tutorialTitle: Selecting replacement ewes from your own flock
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Which ewe lambs to retain and what to look for.
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Breeding your own replacements gives you control over flock genetics, reduces biosecurity risk from bought-in stock, and makes the flock incrementally more suited to your land. entries.tutorialTitle: Fallen stock and disposal options in the UK
entries.tutorialSubtitle: What to do with an animal that has died on the holding.
entries.tutorialExcerpt: An animal that dies on your holding is fallen stock. UK law requires it to be disposed of by an approved route within a specific time. Burial is not permitted for most species. The approved routes are collection by a licensed knacker or renderer, a fallen stock service, or incineration at an approved facility.

entries.tutorialTitle: Getting a soil test for your pasture
entries.tutorialSubtitle: How to sample, where to send it, and what the results mean.
entries.tutorialExcerpt: A soil test for pasture measures pH, phosphorus, potassium, and magnesium, the four factors that most commonly limit grass productivity on smallholdings. The result tells you whether to lime, what fertiliser to apply, and whether the pasture is capable of supporting the stocking rate you intend.

entries.tutorialTitle: Selecting a gilt for breeding
entries.tutorialSubtitle: What to look for before keeping a female pig back from slaughter.
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Not every female pig from a litter is worth breeding from. A gilt intended for the breeding herd needs more teats than the average, a sound enough structure to carry multiple pregnancies, and a temperament that will allow easy handling at farrowing. Selecting for these traits takes twenty minutes and saves months of management problems.

entries.tutorialTitle: Goat bloat: recognition and first response
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Identifying frothy and free-gas bloat and what to do before the vet arrives.
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Bloat in goats builds fast and can be fatal within hours if not treated. The left flank distends above the hipbone, the animal is uncomfortable and stops eating, and in severe cases she may have difficulty breathing. Knowing the type of bloat and acting promptly gives the vet the best chance of success.
